1:
The chapter opens on the last king of the ten tribes, and the witnesses meet at once the chronological knot his accession presents. The date given is the twelfth year of Ahaz, yet Hoshea had cut his way to the throne years before. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the scene: here is the reign and ruin of Hoshea, who forced his way to the crown by treason and murder — the conspiracy against Pekah recorded at 2 Kings 15:30 — yet did not gain quiet possession of it until seven or eight years after, for he slew Pekah in the fourth year of Ahaz but did not begin to reign until the twelfth. Whether he was kept from the throne by the king of Assyria, the king of Judah, or some faction of his own people, Henry cannot say; but he reads the delay as a just chastisement and a fulfilment of the prophet's word, “Now they shall say, We have no king, because we feared not the LORD” (Hosea 10:3).

The reformed John Gill wrestles with the same difficulty from the annals. Since Hoshea conspired against Pekah in the twentieth year of Jotham, that is, the fourth of Ahaz, one might expect his reign dated from then; Gill proposes either an interregnum until the twelfth of Ahaz, or that Hoshea, being at first a tributary and a kind of viceroy under the king of Assyria, was not generally acknowledged as king, and so is not reckoned to reign until he rebelled. After that he reigned nine years, four under Ahaz and five under Hezekiah — a reckoning Gill takes from the Jewish chronologers.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott cuts the knot with the numbers of the surrounding reigns: if Pekah reigned thirty years and Ahaz succeeded in Pekah's seventeenth, then Ahaz ran thirteen years concurrently with Pekah, so that Hoshea succeeded in the fourteenth year of Ahaz. He grounds the accession in the inscription of Tiglath-Pileser, according to which Hoshea (A-u-si-ha) mounted the throne only as a vassal of Assyria, and probably refused further tribute on the news of Tiglath's death. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley harmonizes it his own way: Hoshea usurped the kingdom in Ahaz's fourth year but was not accepted and settled until the twelfth, and the “nine years” are counted from that confirmation, so that in all he reigned some seventeen or eighteen years. The voices thus divide over the arithmetic — interregnum, vassalage, or concurrent reckoning — while agreeing on the melancholy substance: the man who at last sits secure on Samaria's throne is the last king who ever will.
2:
The standing verdict of the northern kings falls on Hoshea too — “he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD” — but with a startling qualification the witnesses cannot pass over: “but not as the kings of Israel that were before him.” The reformed John Gill takes the qualification concretely. Hoshea did not worship Baal, as some of his predecessors had done, and he could not worship the calves, for these had already been carried off by the Assyrians in the earlier deportations, as the Jews say; Gill adds the tradition that Hoshea removed the garrisons his forerunners had set on the borders to keep the people from going up to Jerusalem, so that the fifteenth of the month Ab was afterward kept as a festival. Yet the captivity fell precisely in this comparatively better reign, to show that the ruin was not to be charged on the kings alone but on the people's own sins.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the same point with more caution. The evaluating phrase is used of every northern king but Shallum, who reigned only a month; we can hardly assume that Hoshea abandoned the calf-worship of Bethel, but he may well have discountenanced the cult of the Baals and Asheras. Ellicott cites the Seder Olam, which holds that Hoshea did not replace the calf of Bethel — already carried off, it assumes, in fulfilment of the prophecy of Hosea 10:5 — and he draws the shrewd historical parallel that the last sovereigns of a falling monarchy are not always the worst of their line, instancing Charles I and Louis XVI.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gathers the threads into a single reckoning. Hoshea neither worshipped Baal, as many of his predecessors did, nor compelled the people to worship the calves — one of them, that of Dan, being destroyed or carried away before, as the Hebrew writers affirm — nor, some add, did he hinder by force those who were minded to go up to Jerusalem. And yet, Wesley concludes, the measure of Israel's sins being now full, vengeance comes upon them without remedy. The witnesses converge on the sober irony the historian intends: judgment need not wait for the worst of kings, and a nation's guilt, long treasured up, may break at last over a reign in which the throne itself had grown a shade less wicked than before.
3:
The instrument of judgment now appears — “against him came up Shalmaneser king of Assyria; and Hoshea became his servant, and gave him presents” — and the witnesses pause first to fix the identity of this Assyrian. The reformed John Gill weighs whether Shalmaneser is the same as Tiglath-Pileser, and concludes he is rather his son; he notes that Josephus found the name in the archives of the Tyrians, against whom Shalmaneser waged war, and that Metasthenes gives him a reign of seventeen years. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees that he was the son and successor of Tiglath-Pileser, the same called Shalman by the prophet in Hosea 10:14 and Enemessar in the book of Tobit; the “presents” mean simply that Hoshea became tributary.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott brings the cuneiform record to bear. Shalmaneser IV, whose name he renders “Shalman be gracious,” was the successor of Tiglath-Pileser II and predecessor of Sargon, reigning 727 to 722 B.C. No annals of his survive, but a fragment of the Eponym-list notes foreign expeditions in the three years 725 to 723; and this agrees with Menander, in Josephus, according to whom Shalmaneser led an expedition against Tyre — and no doubt against Israel, as Tyre's ally — that lasted five years, running on past his own reign into that of Sargon. Nothing, Ellicott adds, is known of how Shalmaneser died.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley records a rival identification he does not himself adopt: the ancient Hebrew writers made Shalmaneser the same person as Sennacherib, who eight years later invaded Judah, it being frequent in the East for one man to bear several names. Wesley too appeals to Josephus and the Tyrian annals, and explains the campaign either as punishment for tribute denied or as a means of making Hoshea tributary in the first place. So the witnesses agree that a great Assyrian has come up against the doomed kingdom, and that Hoshea has bought a reprieve with vassalage and tribute; they differ only over the king's precise place in the Assyrian succession — son of Tiglath-Pileser to Gill, Clarke, and Ellicott, but confused by the older rabbis with the later Sennacherib. The reprieve, in any case, is bought at the price of servitude, and the next verse will show how quickly Hoshea tires of the bargain.
4:
Hoshea's vassalage does not hold: “the king of Assyria found conspiracy in Hoshea; for he had sent messengers to So king of Egypt, and brought no present to the king of Assyria.” The reformed John Gill reads it as a scheme of revolt detected before it ripened, the Assyrian learning of it very probably through spies he had set to watch his tributary. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the plot plainly: Hoshea had endeavoured to shake off the Assyrian yoke by entering a treaty with So, king of Egypt, and having done so ceased to send the annual tribute.

The grammatical-historical witnesses labour over the two disputed names in the verse. On “conspiracy,” Charles Ellicott explains that it was a conspiracy with Egypt against Hoshea's suzerain, and reports that Thenius wished to read “falsehood” after the Septuagint, a change requiring only the transposition of two Hebrew letters — but Ellicott judges the change needless. On “So,” he and Keil and Delitzsch both wrestle with the identification. Keil and Delitzsch take So to be one of the two Shebeks of the twenty-fifth Ethiopian dynasty, though they canvass at length whether it was Sevechus or his father Sabako. Ellicott repoints the name as Seveh or Sewe, corresponding to the Assyrian Shab'i and the Egyptian Shabaka, the Greek Sabaco, first king of the Ethiopian dynasty, whom Sargon defeated at Raphia in 720 B.C.; he stresses that Sargon calls him only “prince” rather than “king,” for at this time Lower Egypt was split among petty principalities, so that an Egyptian alliance was of little worth — the very broken reed of Isaiah 19:20.

On the closing clause the witnesses read a significant detail of order. Ellicott notes that “brought no present” is better “offered no present,” the word elsewhere used of sacrifice, and that Hoshea failed to discharge the regular yearly due. And the statement that the king of Assyria “shut him up, and bound him in prison” seems to imply, Ellicott observes, that Shalmaneser took Hoshea captive before the siege of Samaria — a supposition supported by the fact that Sargon, who ended the siege, makes no mention of capturing or killing the Israelite king. The witnesses thus leave Hoshea disarmed at the outset: a vassal who gambled on Egypt, lost his gamble, and vanished into an Assyrian prison while his capital still stood and its ordeal had scarcely begun.
5:
With the king imprisoned, the land itself is overrun: “then the king of Assyria came up throughout all the land, and went up to Samaria, and besieged it three years.” The reformed John Gill notes how unresisted the invasion was — there was none to oppose the Assyrian's march, Hoshea not daring to come out and meet him in the field. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the length of the siege as a measure of the city's strength: to have held out three years, Samaria must have been well fortified, well provisioned, and well defended.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the event in its exact frame. The Assyrian came up throughout the whole land, advanced upon the capital, and besieged it three years; and the ninth year of Hoshea, in which the city fell, answers to the sixth year of Hezekiah and to the year 722 or 721 B.C., in which the kingdom of the ten tribes was destroyed. They begin already here the vexed question of where the exiles were carried, insisting from the plain words of the text that the places named lay not in Mesopotamia but in the provinces of Assyria and Media.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, raises the delicate historical problem the bare narrative glides over: which Assyrian king actually took Samaria? Sargon, in his own inscription, states that he captured Samaria in his first year, which means Shalmaneser had besieged the city some two years before his death. The scriptural account, Ellicott observes, does not discriminate between the respective shares of the two Assyrian sovereigns in the overthrow of the kingdom; and it is noticeable that the text does not actually say that Shalmaneser “besieged Samaria three years” and himself “took” it, leaving room for the change of hands that the monuments record. So the witnesses hold two things together: the certainty of the three-year siege and the fall in Hoshea's ninth year, and the quieter uncertainty over whether the crown of that grim conquest belongs to Shalmaneser, who began it, or to Sargon, who finished it. What none of them doubts is that the walls which had stood for three years were about to come down, and with them a kingdom two centuries old.
6:
The blow falls: “in the ninth year of Hoshea the king of Assyria took Samaria, and carried Israel away into Assyria, and placed them in Halah, and in Habor by the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes.” The reformed John Gill notes that this ninth year was the last of the reign, reckoned either from Hoshea's coming to full authority or from his casting off the Assyrian yoke. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sees the fall foretold by the prophets in Hosea 13:16 and Micah 1:6, and reckons that the kingdom of Israel had lasted two hundred and fifty-four years, from the schism of Jeroboam to this capture.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott brings the Assyrian record to the verse. In the great inscription published by Botta, Sargon boasts, “The city of Samaria I assaulted, I took; 27,280 men dwelling in the midst thereof I carried off; 50 chariots among them I set apart, and the rest of their wealth I let my soldiers take.” Ellicott locates Halah with the Halahhu of the Assyrian lists, corrects “Habor by the river of Gozan” to “on Habor the river of Gozan,” and connects the places with the note on 1 Chronicles 5:26.

Over the fate of the exiles the witnesses openly divide. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley take the mournful, mainstream view: carried into Assyria, the ten tribes were in time so mingled with the nations that they were lost, and the name of Israel was no more in remembrance — “they that forgot God were themselves forgotten” — though a remnant escaped into Judah, so that James could write to the twelve tribes scattered abroad. Clarke, however, will not let the tribes vanish. He devotes a long note to tracing them, and, citing Sir William Jones on the history of the Afghans — their own tradition of Jewish descent, their tribal names, the resemblance of Pushtoo to Chaldaic, and a district called Hazaret that might answer to the Arsaret of Esdras — he judges it “by far the most probable” that the Afghans are the descendants of the Israelites led away by the Assyrian kings. So the debate is genuine: were the ten tribes absorbed and effaced among the heathen, as Henry and Wesley soberly conclude, or do they survive, traceable, in some far quarter of the East, as Clarke's speculation would have it? The witnesses agree only on the fact of the deportation — a whole people uprooted — and part company the moment they ask what became of them afterward.
7:
Here the historian lays down the sword of narrative and takes up the scalpel of judgment. “For so it was, that the children of Israel had sinned against the LORD their God” — and the witnesses recognise that a long theological indictment has begun. The reformed John Gill states its theme in a word: the sin here enlarged upon as the cause of the captivity is idolatry. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the grammar, reading the opening as “and it came to pass when” rather than “because,” with the true apodosis delayed until the sentence of removal; the whole of verses seven through seventeen is a single explanation of Israel's sin, and it opens by recalling the redemption from Egypt, that the magnitude of the ingratitude might appear.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the passage its fullest meditation, and insists that the ruin must be traced not to second causes but to the First Cause. It was the LORD who removed Israel out of his sight; whoever were the instruments, he was the author of the calamity, the Assyrian being merely the rod of his anger, as in Isaiah 10:5. Yet this was no arbitrary act of sovereignty but one of necessary justice, for by their own way and doings they procured it all to themselves. Henry then unfolds what God had done to bind them to himself — he gave them their liberty, brought them from under the hand of Pharaoh and owned Israel as his son; he gave them their law and was himself their king; he gave them their land, casting out the heathen before them.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott grounds the same point in the covenant's own preamble: Jehovah's claim to Israel's exclusive fealty rested from the outset on the fact that he had emancipated them from Egyptian bondage, the very ground asserted in Exodus 20:2 and echoed in Hosea 11:1. The “other gods” they feared he identifies as the Baals and Asheras of Canaan, symbols of nature's productive powers, and further the heavenly bodies, the worship rebuked in Amos 5:26 and Ezekiel 8:16. The witnesses do not divide. Together they frame the verse as the hinge of the chapter: the fall just narrated was no accident of politics or arms but the righteous recompense of a redeemed people who feared the gods of their neighbours instead of the God who had brought them out of Egypt.
8:
The indictment now specifies the pattern of the apostasy: “and walked in the statutes of the heathen, whom the LORD cast out from before the children of Israel, and of the kings of Israel, which they had made.” The reformed John Gill marks the sharp aggravation in it. They walked in the very idolatrous ways of the Canaanites, whose ejection from the land ought to have stood as a standing warning; and they were the more inexcusable because they had been expressly cautioned against these customs in the law of Leviticus 18:3.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out the two-fold direction of the guilt the verse names. Israel's apostasy showed itself first in walking in the statutes of the nations the LORD had cut off before them, instead of in the statutes of Jehovah, contrary to the express command of Leviticus 20:23; and second in walking in the statutes which the kings of Israel had made — that is, the worship of the calves. They note the syntax by which the relative clause “which they had made” refers back to the kings, as the parallel in v.19 makes plain.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, sharpens the same distinction into a definition of the national guilt. It was two things at once, he says. First, idolatry in the strict sense — the worship of other gods than Jehovah, the Baals and Asheras of the heathen round about. Second, a heathenish mode of worshipping Jehovah himself — namely, under the form of a bullock, as Jeroboam the First had ordained. The word “statutes,” Ellicott adds, means religious rules or ordinances, and the closing clause points to the statutes the kings of Israel had made, the same charge repeated in v.19. The witnesses stand wholly together. They see in the verse a double corruption: the importing of the abominations of the dispossessed Canaanites, and the state-sponsored calf-cult of the northern throne — the one a treason against the God of the covenant, the other a counterfeit of his worship. Both flowed from the same refusal to keep the way marked out in the law, and both, the witnesses agree, were freely chosen where the fate of the Canaanites had left them without excuse.
9:
The catalogue of sins turns to their manner — “and the children of Israel did secretly those things that were not right against the LORD their God” — and here the witnesses divide sharply over a single obscure Hebrew verb. The reformed John Gill takes the received sense: they did these things secretly, whether because they were ashamed to commit them openly, or because they foolishly imagined that what was done in private escaped the eye of an all-seeing God, having imbibed an atheistical notion that he did not see; or the word may mean they “covered” their idolatry under fair pretences too thin to hide it. The wesleyan-arminian voices largely follow this reading. Adam Clarke finds much hidden iniquity and private idolatry among them, while the added phrase “from the tower of the watchmen to the fenced city” shows the idolatry was also universal, filling every place from the largest city to the smallest shepherd's cot. John Wesley allows both senses — either gross practices they were ashamed to own, or the calf-worship they “covered” with fair pretences of political necessity and honest intention toward the true God.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reject the whole line. The verb, they argue with Hengstenberg, means “they covered words which were not right concerning Jehovah their God” — that is, they sought to conceal the true nature of Jehovah by arbitrary perversions of his word, worshipping him in ways of their own invention and so making him like the idols. They expressly set aside the alternatives: Thenius's “they trifled with things not right,” and Gesenius's “they acted perfidiously,” both being at variance with the usage of the language. Charles Ellicott stands nearer to Keil here, noting that the literal sense is “covered,” and that Hebrew verbs of covering and hiding are often used for dealing perfidiously or deceitfully; the form in the text occurs only here.

On the high places all agree. Ellicott sees in them the first institution of unlawful places of worship; the towers named are like the solitary watch-towers of 2 Chronicles 26:10, contrasted with the embattled cities, so that wherever men were gathered, in small numbers or great, these shrines were set up. The witnesses thus leave the verse doubly indicting: whether Israel sinned in secret, as Gill and Clarke hold, or laboured rather to disguise the very character of their God, as Keil contends, they had planted altars from the loneliest tower to the strongest city, and covered the whole land with a worship that was “not right.”
10:
The idolatrous furniture is now named: “and they set them up images and groves in every high hill, and under every green tree.” The reformed John Gill attends first to the plain difficulty of the words. Since groves of trees cannot be set up under green trees, he takes the term of images and idols of stone and wood, placed in such shaded spots as the heathen loved; he even reaches for a contemporary parallel, noting how the Indians in his own day dispersed their idols here and there in the fields, in little groves or at the foot of some shadowing hill.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the setting up of monuments and asherim on every high hill and under every green tree — a practice already condemned in the days of Jeroboam at 1 Kings 14:23. They observe, importantly, that in this whole description of the idolatry the historian has in mind not the ten tribes only but Judah as well, as the mention of both kingdoms at v.13 and v.19 shows. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke identifies the objects as images of different idols, and the places as those set apart for the abominable rites of Ashtaroth or Venus.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, sharpens the rendering: not “images and groves” but “pillars and Asheras” — sacred trunks — and he numbers this as the second degree of guilt, the setting up of idolatrous symbols after the first sin of instituting the high places themselves. The witnesses do not contend. Between them they build up the picture the verse intends: a land whose every prominent hill and every spreading tree had been claimed for a shrine, its slopes crowded with standing pillars and wooden Asheras, the emblems of a fertility-cult borrowed from the very Canaanites God had driven out. What Gill illustrates from the idol-strewn fields of India, Keil and Ellicott trace to the ancient warnings of the law; and all agree that this was no scattered or occasional lapse but a systematic covering of the country with the apparatus of false worship, so that there was scarcely a height or a shade in Israel that had not been turned to the service of another god.
11:
The indictment moves from the shrines to the smoke that rose from them: “and there they burnt incense in all the high places, as did the heathen whom the LORD carried away before them; and wrought wicked things to provoke the LORD to anger.” The reformed John Gill notes tersely that in this they did as even the tribe of Judah did, a corruption observed in all the preceding reigns, so that the sin was not the north's alone.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the terrible comparison in the verse: Israel burned incense on all the high places “like the nations which Jehovah drove out before them.” They point to the sombre irony buried in the Hebrew, that the word rendered “carried away” means literally “to lead into exile,” here applied to the expulsion and destruction of the Canaanites — the very fate now overtaking Israel. The people had made themselves like the dispossessed nations in worship, and so came to share the dispossession; the same term that describes the Canaanites' removal hangs over Israel's own banishment. The judgment fits the crime with a dreadful exactness: those who copied the idolatry of the heathen inherited the heathen's ruin.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, presses the closing clause, “and wrought wicked things to provoke the LORD to anger,” beyond the merely ceremonial. These were not idolatrous rites only, he insists, but the hideous immoralities which constituted a recognised part of the nature-worships of Canaan — the cult and its corruptions being of one piece. The witnesses do not divide. Together they read the verse as the joining of false worship to open provocation: incense burned on every hill in imitation of the very nations God had cast out, and with it the moral degradation that such worship carried in its train. Gill's quiet reminder that Judah was tainted too widens the guilt; Keil's note on the language deepens its justice; and Ellicott's warning about the immoralities strips the idolatry of any claim to be a harmless matter of ritual. The high places smoked not with innocent devotion but with a worship that was at once an insult to the God of the covenant and a school of the vices for which he had already emptied the land once.
12:
The historian names the objects of this worship with a word of contempt: “for they served idols, whereof the LORD had said unto them, Ye shall not do this thing.” The reformed John Gill hears the scorn in the Hebrew term, which he renders “dunghill gods,” as the word often signifies in Scripture; he even recalls the pagan Sterculius, a name of Saturn supposed to be drawn from the finding out of manure, so debased were the deities to which Israel bowed.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the same contemptuous sense with precision: they “served the clods,” worshipping masses of stone or lumps of matter as gods — the word gillulim, they note, connecting it with its use at 1 Kings 15:12 — and all this in flat defiance of the plain command of God in Exodus 20:3 and Leviticus 26:1. The point the verse itself makes is that the sin was committed against express prohibition: the LORD had said, “Ye shall not do this thing,” and they did it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott joins them in the rendering, correcting “they served idols” to “they served the dunglings,” a term of contempt he traces through 1 Kings 15:19 and Deuteronomy 29:16.

The witnesses stand entirely together on this verse, and their agreement is itself instructive. Every one of them seizes on the same feature — the sheer contemptibleness of the gods, “clods,” “dunglings,” “dunghill gods” — and sets it against the express, remembered word of the LORD who had forbidden them. The tragedy the historian means the reader to feel is the disproportion: a redeemed people, warned in the very words of the Decalogue and the covenant law, exchanged the living God who brought them out of Egypt for lumps of shaped stone. There is no debate to record here, and the unanimity is the message. What Gill mocks with Sterculius, Keil grounds in the lexicon, and Ellicott in the syntax; but all three drive at the one truth, that the second commandment was known to Israel, quoted against Israel, and trampled by Israel, so that the coming judgment could charge them with no ignorance but only with a deliberate and warned rebellion.
13:
Against this long apostasy the LORD is not silent: “yet the LORD testified against Israel, and against Judah, by all the prophets, and by all the seers, saying, Turn ye from your evil ways.” The reformed John Gill catalogues the messengers — the LORD testified against their sins, reproving, dehorting, exhorting them to repent, in every preceding reign, by Ahijah the Shilonite, by Elijah and Elisha, by Hosea, Amos, and Micah, and others. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sees in this the aggravation of their guilt: what rendered their conduct the more inexcusable was that God preserved among them a continual succession of prophets who testified against their idolatry, preached repentance, and held out his readiness to forgive if only they would return.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add that God was not satisfied with the mere prohibitions of the law, but bore living witness through all his prophets, who pressed Israel and Judah to turn and obey — all in vain, for they were stiff-necked like their fathers. They pause over why Judah is named here at all, and answer, against Thenius, that it is not because the passage looks back to the time before the kingdom was divided, but because the historian means to signal beforehand that Judah was already preparing the same fate for herself, as v.19 will say more plainly.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, presses both the verb and the versions. “Testified against” he renders “adjured” or gave solemn warning and charge, a sense repeated in v.15 with its cognate noun. On “by all the prophets, and by all the seers” he weighs the variant readings of the manuscripts, the Targum, the Syriac, and the Vulgate, and defines the seers as men who, without belonging to the prophetic order, came forward in times of emergency upon a sudden divine impulse; the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees, describing the seers as those to whom God revealed his mind by visions and who bore witness by glorious miracles. The witnesses converge on the mercy that shines through the indictment: the God who was about to remove Israel had first, for generations and by every kind of messenger, pleaded with her to turn — so that the judgment, when it fell, fell on a people who had been warned to the last.
14:
The plea of the prophets meets a wall: “notwithstanding they would not hear, but hardened their necks, like to the neck of their fathers, that did not believe in the LORD their God.” The reformed John Gill states it simply — they would not hear the instruction, advice, and admonition of the prophets, nor obey it. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the metaphor: to harden the neck is to refuse to submit the neck to the yoke of God's precepts, a figure taken from stubborn oxen that will not bow to the yoke.

The grammatical-historical witnesses press the diagnosis deeper. Charles Ellicott notes that the Hebrew has “neck” in the singular, as in Deuteronomy 10:16 and Jeremiah 17:23, and that where the Septuagint and Syriac read “more than the neck of their fathers” the difference is but a single Hebrew letter. Above all he insists that the closing charge — they “did not believe” — refers not to intellectual but to moral unbelief, an unbelief that evinced itself as disobedience; they did not render the obedience of faith. Keil and Delitzsch carry the same thread into the next verse, where the fathers' unbelief flowers into open rejection: they spurned God's statutes and his covenant, “followed vanity and became vain,” the very words of Jeremiah 2:5, and went after the surrounding heathen whom the LORD had charged them not to imitate.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gains its weight from their agreement on its root. The failure was not first of the intellect but of the will and the heart — a stubbornness inherited, “like to the neck of their fathers,” so that the sin of the wilderness generation is seen running in the blood of their children. Gill and Wesley picture the ox that will not bend; Ellicott names the moral unbelief beneath the stiffened neck; Keil traces where such unbelief inevitably leads. Together they show why the tireless testimony of the prophets bore no fruit: the word came faithfully and often, but it fell on a people who had set themselves, generation after generation, not to believe and not to obey. The tragedy is not that Israel was untaught, but that Israel would not hear.
15:
The stubbornness of the fathers now unfolds into open repudiation: “and they rejected his statutes, and his covenant that he made with their fathers, and his testimonies which he testified against them; and they followed vanity, and became vain.” The reformed John Gill fixes the covenant they cast off as the one God made at Sinai and Horeb — the solemn bond of their national life, flung away.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers on the haunting phrase “they followed vanity, and became vain,” the same expression that stands in Jeremiah 2:5. The word “vanity,” he explains, carries the article and means strictly a breath, and then that which is as transient as a breath, as in Job 7:16; here it denotes the idols and their worship. The cognate verb “became vain” means dealt or talked foolishly, and answers to the Greek of Romans 1:21. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley presses the same word to its moral edge: idols are called “vanity” because of their nothingness, impotency, and unprofitableness; and by the long worship of idols the people were themselves made like them — vain, sottish, and senseless creatures.

Here the witnesses uncover the deep law that governs the whole indictment: men become like what they worship. The verse sets two clauses side by side — “they followed vanity, and became vain” — and Ellicott and Wesley alike seize the terrible logic that joins them. To pursue an empty thing is to be emptied; to serve a breath is to become a breath; to bow to the nothing-gods is to be hollowed out into their likeness. The rejection is total, mounting through the verse from statutes to covenant to testimonies, each a further tearing of the bond that v.13 had described God as tirelessly upholding. And the going after the heathen round about is precisely the imitation the law had forbidden. The witnesses do not contend; they let the verse's own doubled phrase preach its warning, that a people who reject the living God for the works of their hands do not merely sin against reason but lose it, following vanity until they are themselves grown vain.
16:
The apostasy reaches its summit: “and they left all the commandments of the LORD their God, and made them molten images, even two calves, and made a grove, and worshipped all the host of heaven, and served Baal.” The reformed John Gill reads the first clause as the root of all the rest — they left all the commandments, for, he adds with the apostle, he that offends in one point is guilty of all, as James 2:10 declares.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch call this the very climax of the apostasy, and unfold its several idolatries. The molten images are the two golden calves of 1 Kings 12:28; the “grove” is the Asherah, the idol of Astarte set up as at 1 Kings 16:33; the worship of “all the host of heaven” — sun, moon, and stars — and the service of Baal belong to the days of Ahab and his house. They note a nice historical point: the worship of the heavenly host is not elsewhere recorded of the northern kingdom and appears expressly first in Judah under Manasseh at 2 Kings 21:3, so that its mention here, drawn from the language of Deuteronomy 4:19, describes the general character of the Baal and Astarte worship.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott devotes his fullest note to the Asherah, citing Schlottmann at length. Asherah, he argues, was but another name for the supreme goddess Ashtoreth, as the parallel of “Baal and Ashtaroth” in Judges 2:13 with “Baal and Asherim” in Judges 3:7 shows; the Asherah itself was a real trunk of a tree, planted rootless but not branchless, signifying nature as goddess beside Baal the lord of the world, akin to the Assyrian sacred tree and the Norse Yggdrasil. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the verse as a descent by degrees — from a partial disobedience they fell to a total apostasy, leaving all — and glosses “the host” as the stars, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, and Venus. The witnesses do not divide. They see the verse gathering every strand of false worship — calves, Asherah, star-host, and Baal — into one full picture of a nation that had abandoned the whole law of God for the entire pantheon of its neighbours.
17:
The catalogue descends to its darkest rites: “and they caused their sons and their daughters to pass through the fire, and used divination and enchantments, and sold themselves to do evil in the sight of the LORD.” The reformed John Gill reads the passing through the fire as an offering to Baal or Moloch, whom he takes to be the same and to represent the sun that presides in the element of fire, done either by way of lustration or unto actual burning.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott names the rites with precision: the passing of children through the fire is the cultus of Moloch, as at 2 Kings 16:3; the divination and enchantments are the forbidden arts of Deuteronomy 18:10 and Numbers 23:23; and the phrase “sold themselves” regards idolatry as a servitude, as in 1 Kings 21:20. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke seizes on that same figure with force: to sell oneself is to be abandoned to the will of the devil, to work all iniquity with greediness.

Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical voice, carry the sentence to its dread conclusion in the next verse. This whole conduct, they say, excited the anger of God, so that he removed Israel from his face and left only the tribe — that is, the kingdom — of Judah; and even Judah did not keep the commandments but walked in the statutes of Israel, the idolatry of the house of Ahab under Joram, Ahaziah, and Ahaz, so that the parenthesis of v.19 is already in view. The witnesses do not divide. They read the verse as the last and lowest rung of the descent that began with the high places: from unlawful shrines to pillars, from pillars to calves and Baal, and now to the burning of their own children and the trafficking in sorcery, until the people had, in the historian's fearful phrase, sold themselves to evil. The self-sale is the key the witnesses hold in common — a people not merely tempted into sin but bound over to it, so wholly given up that nothing remained but the anger of God, to which the next verse turns.
18:
The long indictment reaches its verdict: “therefore the LORD was very angry with Israel, and removed them out of his sight: there was none left but the tribe of Judah only.” The reformed John Gill fixes on the cause of the wrath, for nothing is more provoking to God than idolatry, and this was the sin that had filled the whole preceding catalogue.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke unfolds what the removal meant. To be removed out of God's sight was to be banished from the promised land, from the temple, and from every ordinance of righteousness, as wholly unworthy of any kind of good. And on the sombre clause “none left but the tribe of Judah only,” Clarke and Wesley together widen the reckoning: under the name of Judah are comprised also Benjamin and Levi, and those Israelites of the other tribes who had abandoned their idolatries and joined themselves to Judah; it was the ten tribes that the Assyrians carried away. Wesley reckons the remnant as the greatest part of Benjamin, with those of Simeon and Levi who were incorporated with them.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fix the sense of the words. Charles Ellicott explains “removed them out of his sight” as banishing them from his land, an expression founded on the old local conceptions of deity, and points forward to v.23; and he takes “the tribe” to mean the kingdom, as in 1 Kings 11:36. Keil and Delitzsch note that the verse is the true apodosis toward which the whole passage from verse seven has been moving — the anger, the removal, and the sparing of Judah alone — even while Judah too had deserved rejection, a qualification that opens into the parenthesis of the next verse. The witnesses do not divide. They read the verse as the historian's formal sentence upon the northern kingdom: idolatry has provoked the very anger the prophets warned of, and the God who brought Israel into his sight in the Exodus now puts them out of it, leaving standing only the one southern kingdom — and that, as the next verses will show, on borrowed time.
19:
The historian breaks his account of the north to enter a warning against the south: “also Judah kept not the commandments of the LORD their God, but walked in the statutes of Israel which they made.” The reformed John Gill reads it as both infection and aggravation: Judah was infected with the idolatry of the ten tribes and drawn into it by their example, and persisted in it even after seeing what befell them — a double guilt shared between the tribes that led and the tribe that followed.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the verse as a deliberate parenthesis, occasioned by the words “only the tribe of Judah” in v.18. The statutes of Israel in which Judah walked, they explain, were not only the Baal-worship taken up under the house of Ahab but also the worship of the high places and of idols practised under many of the kings of Judah — so that the southern kingdom had no clean standing to plead.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott states the point with unsparing clearness: Judah was no real or permanent exception to the sins and the punishment of Israel; she imitated the apostasy of her sister-kingdom and was visited with a like penalty. The “statutes of Israel which they made” he compares with the “statutes of Omri” in Micah 6:16, and observes that Ahaziah and Ahaz especially favoured the northern idolatry, so that the example of her more powerful neighbour cast a fatally powerful spell over Judah. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the moral edge: Judah's wickedness is remembered here as an aggravation of Israel's sin, which was not only evil in itself but mischievous to its neighbour, whom its evil example instructed and provoked. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the verse is a hinge and a threat: even as the north falls, the historian turns to warn the reader that Judah has learned the same lessons, walked the same statutes, and stands under the same sentence — the sparing of verse eighteen being a reprieve, not an acquittal. Gill marks the double aggravation: the north taught the sin, and the south learned it where the fresh ruin of her sister should have taught her rather to fear; so the guilt of both kingdoms is made the heavier, and the reader is left to see in the exile of Israel a rehearsal of the judgment already gathering over Jerusalem.
20:
The parenthesis on Judah closes and the sentence on Israel resumes: “and the LORD rejected all the seed of Israel, and afflicted them, and delivered them into the hand of spoilers, until he had cast them out of his sight.” The reformed John Gill reads the rejection as a divorce: the LORD cast off the ten tribes with loathing and contempt, wrote a “Lo-ammi” upon them, gave them a bill of divorcement, and declared them no more under his care.

Here the witnesses part company over the reach of the phrase “all the seed of Israel.” Gill takes it strictly of the ten tribes. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that “Israel” is used in the narrow sense in these verses, the writer going back to v.18 after the parenthesis about Judah; the “spoilers” he identifies as the Syrians of 2 Kings 10:32 and the Assyrians of 2 Kings 15:29, the writer probably remembering the language of Judges 2:14. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley likewise reads “all Israel” of all the tribes of Israel, first one part and then the rest, though not of every individual, since many had removed into Judah. But the other grammatical-historical voice, Keil and Delitzsch, reads the phrase more widely: “all the seed of Israel” means not merely the ten tribes but the whole nation, humbled until it was thrust from God's face.

The debate is real, but the witnesses converge on a distinction that softens it. Keil observes that the word “rejected” differs from the phrase “cast out of his sight”: the latter denotes the driving into exile, while the former names that gentler rejection which consisted in chastisement and delivering into the hand of plunderers — penal judgments by which the LORD sought to lead Israel and Judah alike to turn to him, and so to spare them from being driven among the heathen. On this reading the verse holds two movements: a long history of afflictions meant to reclaim, and, when these failed, the final casting out. Whether “all the seed of Israel” be the ten tribes only, as Gill and Ellicott hold, or the whole covenant nation, as Keil contends, the witnesses agree that the rejection was neither hasty nor loveless, but the last act of a God who had chastened long before he cast away.
21:
The historian traces the ruin back to its fountainhead: “for he rent Israel from the house of David; and they made Jeroboam the son of Nebat king: and Jeroboam drave Israel from following the LORD, and made them sin a great sin.” The reformed John Gill refers the rending to the days of Rehoboam, when the ten tribes revolted, the kingdom torn like a garment into twelve pieces with ten given to Jeroboam; and he ascribes the act to the LORD, as done according to his purpose and prophecy, worked out by his providence.

Here a genuine grammatical debate opens over the subject of the verb. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch argue that it is Jehovah who “rent Israel from the house of David,” not Israel who rent away the kingdom — the latter supposing too harsh an ellipsis, and the thought nowhere else expressed; the reference is to 1 Kings 11:31, where the LORD rends the kingdom from Solomon, while the making of Jeroboam king recalls 1 Kings 12:20. The other grammatical-historical voice, Charles Ellicott, reads the Hebrew differently: as it stands the verb can only mean “Israel rent,” and the want of an object favours Thenius's suggestion that the passive should be restored — “Israel rent himself away” — comparing the Vulgate's scissus est. So the debate is whether God tore Israel from David's house in judgment, or Israel tore itself away in rebellion; the verse is the fountainhead either way, but the grammar leaves the hand uncertain.

On the sequel the witnesses agree. Ellicott sets out the chain of ruin: separation from Judah led to the calf-worship, and the calf-worship to idolatry pure and simple. The word “drave” he takes as “put far away,” as in Amos 2:3, or by the Hebrew margin “misled,” as in 2 Chronicles 21:11. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds a double note: the people's making Jeroboam king was itself a sin, for they would not wait till God's providence gave him the throne he was promised, but rose against the house of David; and Jeroboam “drave” them by keeping them by force from the worship at Jerusalem. The calf-worship is called “a great sin,” Wesley says, to meet the Israelites' fond conceit that it was a small one. The witnesses thus lay the whole two-century tragedy at a single door: the rending of the kingdom and the calves that followed it.
22:
The historian presses the charge home to its persistence: “for the children of Israel walked in all the sins of Jeroboam which he did; they departed not from them.” The reformed John Gill fastens on the two-fold obedience Israel rendered to their first apostate king: they observed his injunction not to go up to Jerusalem to worship, and they worshipped the calves that he had made — the whole national religion shaped by the policy of Jeroboam and never once forsaken.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the fatal continuity to its end. The sons of Israel walked in all the sins of Jeroboam until the LORD removed them from his face and thrust them out of the land, as he had threatened through all his prophets from the time of Jeroboam onward — a threat they document from 1 Kings 14:15 through Hosea 1:6, Amos 5:27, and Isaiah 28:1. They add a careful note on the phrase “unto this day,” observing that the Hebrew particle, like the German bis, is not always exclusive, so that the words prove nothing about whether the exiles ever returned; the expression simply carries the banishment down to the time when the books were written. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sums the verse in a sentence: Israel obstinately persisted in the sin of Jeroboam, in spite of all warning.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse's force lies in the word “departed not.” The sin was not a lapse but a fixed and inherited policy, held to across two centuries and through the mouths of every prophet God sent. Gill shows how thoroughly the calf-cult had become the settled religion of the north, obeyed in its prohibition against Jerusalem as much as in its positive worship; Keil shows how the historian binds the persistence directly to the removal, so that the exile is presented not as a stroke of fortune but as the long-threatened wage of an unrepented sin. The chapter's argument is now complete in its logic: a people that would not depart from Jeroboam's sin was at last made to depart from Jeroboam's land, and the God who had warned them by all his servants the prophets kept, in the end, his every word.
23:
The sentence is executed and the section sealed: “until the LORD removed Israel out of his sight, as he had said by all his servants the prophets. So was Israel carried away out of their own land to Assyria unto this day.” The reformed John Gill glosses it plainly — the LORD suffered them to be carried captive into the land of Assyria, removing them from his presence as the just term of all that had gone before.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott stresses that the removal came “as he had said by all his servants the prophets,” citing Hosea 1:6, Amos 3:11, and Isaiah 28:1. Yet he is careful that the land was not left wholly empty, as 2 Chronicles 30:1 and 2 Chronicles 34:9 show; henceforth, however, the distinctive character of the nation was lost, the Hebrews who remained mixing with their heathen neighbours. He quotes Robertson Smith at length: when Josiah destroyed the northern high places he slew their priests as purely heathen, by contrast with the Judean priests provided for at Jerusalem; the people Hosea and Amos describe were not fitted to keep their identity apart, and, scattered among the nations, accepted strange gods and lost their distinctive existence, so that “the problem of the lost tribes…is a purely fanciful one.”

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward, noting that the “king of Assyria” who now repeoples the land is not Shalmaneser but, by the evidence of Ezra 4:2, a later monarch, since a considerable interval lay between the carrying away and the sending of colonists. The witnesses stand together on the meaning of the moment. Ellicott's sober verdict — that the ten tribes were absorbed and lost, their recovery a fanciful hope — stands squarely against the speculation Clarke had ventured over verse six, and marks the honest division in the sources on the fate of exiled Israel. But on the theology of the verse there is no division at all: the removal was neither accident nor mere conquest, but the exact fulfilment of what God had spoken by all his servants the prophets, so that the empty land itself became a sermon on the faithfulness of God to his own threatening word.
24:
With Israel gone, the land receives new masters: “and the king of Assyria brought men from Babylon, and from Cuthah, and from Ava, and from Hamath, and from Sepharvaim, and placed them in the cities of Samaria.” The reformed John Gill notes that Babylon was then under Assyrian dominion, and weighs whether the king who did this was Shalmaneser or rather Esarhaddon, pointing to Ezra 4:2.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the large lesson: it is sad that the holy land should become a heathen land again — “see what work sin makes” — and he reads the whole resettlement as a providence in which God, having emptied the land for Israel's wickedness, transplanted the surplus of Assyria to possess it and to serve as masters over the Israelites that remained. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the Assyrian policy: to remove one people entirely and substitute another cut off all occasion for insurrection, since the transplanted had no patrimony to fight for; he identifies Cuthah with Cush, whence the Samaritans were later called Cuthaeans, and traces Ava to the Avim expelled by the Caphtorim in Deuteronomy 2:23.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott anchors the verse in Sargon's own inscriptions, which record that in his first year, 721 B.C., he settled a body of conquered Babylonians in the land of Hatti, and in his seventh year located Arab tribes in the city of Samerina; he identifies Kuthah with the Kutie of the monuments, site of a temple of Nergal, and Sepharvaim with Sippar, and notes that the Hebrew “placed them” is the very phrase Sargon uses, and that Esarhaddon later reinforced these colonists, again by the witness of Ezra 4:2. So the witnesses divide over the royal name: Gill and Keil, appealing to Ezra, incline to Esarhaddon; Ellicott, from the cuneiform record, credits Sargon with the first settlement and Esarhaddon with a later reinforcement. But they agree on the substance, which Henry states best: a holy land turned heathen, its cities filled with a patchwork of transplanted peoples carrying their idols with them — the mongrel population out of whom, in the verses to follow, the Samaritans would be made.
25:
The new settlers meet an unlooked-for terror: “and so it was at the beginning of their dwelling there, that they feared not the LORD: therefore the LORD sent lions among them, which slew some of them.” The reformed John Gill marks the strangeness of their impiety — they did not so much as serve the God of the land alongside their own gods, as the heathen usually did in a new country, but worshipped only the idols they had brought with them.

Over the lions the witnesses weigh nature and providence together. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reasons that the land, emptied of its inhabitants, would naturally breed wild beasts, which the superstitious newcomers supposed a plague sent because they knew not how to worship the god of the land; “yet,” he adds, “it is likely that God did send lions as a scourge on this bad people” — holding the natural and the supernatural in one hand. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads it much the same way: in the interval the lions indigenous to the country had multiplied naturally enough, their ravages taken by the colonists as a token of the local deity's wrath — and the sacred writer endorses that interpretation, probably remembering the threat of Leviticus 26:22 and the promise of Exodus 23:29. Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical voice, are firmer still: the lions were sent by Jehovah as a punishment, according to the same word of Leviticus 26:22, because the settlers did not fear him.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley presses the theology home. The judgment fell for their gross neglect and contempt of God, contrary even to the practice of the heathen, who used to worship the gods of the lands where they lived; and by it God asserted his own sovereignty over that land, teaching the newcomers that neither were the Israelites cast out, nor they themselves brought in, by any human valour or strength, but by his providence — and that he who had expelled Israel for neglecting his service could turn out these intruders too, if they sinned as Israel had. The witnesses do not truly divide: whether the lions multiplied by nature or were sent by grace, all agree the hand behind them was God's, reclaiming his land and its worship even from a heathen colony.
26:
The terror drives the colonists to petition their king: “wherefore they spake to the king of Assyria, saying, The nations which thou hast removed, and placed in the cities of Samaria, know not the manner of the God of the land: therefore he hath sent lions among them.” The reformed John Gill notes simply that they made this representation in letters, or by messengers sent for the purpose.

The witnesses fasten on the key phrase, “the manner of the God of the land.” The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the Hebrew word mishpat, “judgment,” as here meaning the way in which the God of the land is to be worshipped — his prescribed rite. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that the word, strictly “judgment” or “decision,” here denotes the appointed worship or cultus, comparing how in the Koran the cognate term “din,” judgment, comes to mean religion; and he corrects the opening, reading not “they spake” but “men spake” — that is, the prefects of the province. Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical voice, set the petition in its train of consequences: the settlers, taking the lions for a punishment from the deity of the land whom they did not worship, asked the king for a priest to teach them the right worship of the God of the land, and so the whole machinery of the coming syncretism was set in motion.

The witnesses do not divide, but the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the scene, draws out its irony. The God of Israel was in truth the God of the whole world, yet these heathen ignorantly call him “the God of the land,” apprehending themselves within his reach and concerned to be on good terms with him. And in this, Henry observes, they shamed the Israelites: the Assyrians were readier to hear the voice of God's judgments than Israel had been, and begged to be taught the very worship that Israel, taught it from their fathers, had despised. The verse, on the witnesses' reading, is thus a mirror held up to the exiled nation — heathen strangers trembling at a stroke of the God whose long-suffering warnings his own people had spurned.
27:
The king answers the petition: “then the king of Assyria commanded, saying, Carry thither one of the priests whom ye brought from thence; and let them go and dwell there, and let him teach them the manner of the God of the land.” The reformed John Gill notes only that these were the orders and directions the king gave in reply.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording, correcting the plural “let them go and dwell” to the singular “let him go and dwell” after the Syriac and Vulgate, and rendering the opening verb “cause to go” and the clause “whom ye brought” as “whom ye carried away.” The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lifts his eyes from the verse to its long outcome. Imperfect as this teaching was, it in the end overthrew the idolatry of these people, so that soon after the Babylonish captivity they were found as free from idolatry as the Jews themselves, and remain so to the present day — though they are now nearly annihilated, a small remnant of some thirty families and about two hundred persons at Naplouse and Jaffa, who keep their Sabbath in white robes and worship in a synagogue; and he directs the curious reader to Sylvestre de Sacy's memoir on the present state of the Samaritans.

The witnesses do not divide, and between them they set the verse in a striking double frame. Read forward, it is the small beginning of the Samaritan people and their worship — a single deported priest sent back to instruct a heathen colony in the rites of Jehovah. Read as Clarke reads it, it is the seed of a strange providence: a half-taught, calf-tainted religion that would nonetheless, through the discipline of the centuries, be purged of its idols until the Samaritans stood as monotheists beside the Jews. The king intends nothing but to quiet his frightened settlers and stop the lions; yet the historian, and Clarke after him, sees the hand of God turning even an Assyrian's administrative order to the slow spread of the knowledge of the true God among the nations that had displaced his people.
28:
The king's order is carried out: “then one of the priests whom they had carried away from Samaria came and dwelt in Bethel, and taught them how they should fear the LORD.” The reformed John Gill gathers what tradition offers of the man — an Arabic writer names him Uzziah, while Epiphanius calls him Esdras, though wrongly making him sent by Nebuchadnezzar thirty years after the Jewish captivity. Gill is sure of one thing: this was one of the priests of the calves, for no others were carried captive from the kingdom of Israel; and his choosing to dwell in Bethel, where he had probably officiated at the calf before, and his teaching the people to make priests of the lowest of them, both betray his origin.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the same reading from the grammar. He renders “taught” as “was teaching,” implying a permanent and settled work, and he too fixes on Bethel as the telling detail — the priest settled there precisely because he was a priest of the calf-worship. And he cautions that “fear the LORD” is meant not in the modern ethical sense but in the ancient ceremonial one: to fear Jehovah is here to observe his outward rite. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees on every point — that the priest taught the manner of God's worship as it was practised in Israel, a conclusion he draws from the man's being an Israelitish priest, from his residence at Bethel, that place infamous for the calves, and from the manner of making priests by his direction.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement carries a quiet warning. The teacher sent to instruct the heathen in the worship of the true God was himself a servant of the corrupted worship — a priest of Jeroboam's calves, settling by old habit in the old shrine, and reproducing among the colonists the very irregularities that had helped ruin Israel. Whatever better knowledge he imparted, he was not likely to teach the nations to do well who had never learned to do well himself. So the Samaritan religion is founded, on the witnesses' account, upon a compromised foundation from its first day: an authentic knowledge of Jehovah, but transmitted through a Bethel priest and shaped from the start toward the mixture the next verses will describe.
29:
The half-taught colonists keep their own gods beside the new one: “howbeit every nation made gods of their own, and put them in the houses of the high places which the Samaritans had made, every nation in their cities wherein they dwelt.” The reformed John Gill reads it as the plain persistence of their imported idolatry: notwithstanding the instructions they had received about the worship of the God of Israel, each nation retained and served its own deities, the work of its own hands, setting them in the high places the Israelites had built, in the room of the images that had stood there before.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds only that every nation made gods after the fashion of those they had worshipped in their own country — the transplanted peoples carrying their ancestral religion with them into the land of their exile. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the theological heart of the matter. The colonists, he insists, did not fear Jehovah in any monotheistic sense; they simply added his cultus to that of their ancestral deities. The “houses of the high places” he identifies as the temples or chapels that had served as the sanctuaries of the several cities in the Samaritan territory, and he notes that “the Samaritans” here means the people of northern Israel, corresponding to the Samaria of v.24.

The witnesses do not divide, and Ellicott's point is the one the verse turns upon. The instruction of the Bethel priest had not displaced the old gods but only made room for one more: Jehovah was received not as the sole God but as an addition to the crowded shelf of national idols. The very shrines Israel had raised in her apostasy now housed the gods of Babylon and Cuthah and Hamath, each nation worshipping in its own city its own deity, and all of them offering the God of the land a share alongside. Here the mongrel worship that the chapter has been moving toward takes visible shape — not the abandonment of Jehovah, which might be honest paganism, but the far subtler corruption of adding him to the pantheon, a corruption the following verses will name god by god.
30:
The historian now names the gods, city by city: “and the men of Babylon made Succothbenoth, and the men of Cuth made Nergal, and the men of Hamath made Ashima.” The witnesses labour over each obscure name, and divide between the traditions of the rabbis and the findings of the monuments. The reformed John Gill canvasses the guesses for Succoth-benoth: the figures of a hen and chickens, according to the Jewish writers; or the Pleiades, the seven stars; or, taking the name as “the booths of the daughters,” the apartments in the temple of Venus or Mylitta where the Babylonian women prostituted themselves once in their lives, a filthy rite Herodotus describes and Valerius Maximus locates at a temple of Sicca Venus.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke follows the same double track. Succoth-benoth he reads as “the tabernacles of the daughters,” the shrine of that Babylonian prostitution in honour of Melitta, and even derives the name Venus from Benoth; Nergal he takes for the solar orb, whose rabbinic emblem was a cock; Ashima, perhaps the fire, from a root meaning to make atonement, in the form of a goat. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets these fancies aside for the Assyriology. The spelling of Succoth-benoth has suffered in transmission, he judges, and the goddess meant is the Babylonian Zirbanit or Zarpanitum, the consort of Merodach; Nergal is the god of the colossal winged lions that guarded the Assyrian doorways, expressly named the god of Kutha; and of Ashima, he confesses, nothing is certainly known, Schrader denying the old identification with the Phoenician Eshmun.

The witnesses thus divide by method rather than by faction: the rabbinic tradition, followed in part by Gill and Clarke, dresses each idol in an animal emblem — hen, cock, goat — while Ellicott, reading the cuneiform record, restores them as known deities of the Babylonian and Assyrian pantheon. The disagreement is real but the moral is shared. Whether these were hens and cocks and goats, or Zarpanitum and Nergal and a forgotten Ashima, they were the gods a heathen people carried into the LORD's land and set up beside his altars — and the very obscurity that now baffles the learned is, as a later witness will say, the fulfilment of the word that these false gods should perish and be buried in oblivion, while the name of the true God endures.
31:
The roll of idols continues to its grimmest entry: “and the Avites made Nibhaz and Tartak, and the Sepharvites burnt their children in fire to Adrammelech and Anammelech, the gods of Sepharvaim.” The reformed John Gill reports the Jewish tradition that Nibhaz was in the shape of a dog, the name drawn from a root meaning to bark, as though it were the barking Anubis of the Egyptians; Tartak he takes for a chain, denoting the fixed stars chained in their places, or the satellites in their orbits, though the rabbis gave it the form of an ass.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report the same rabbinic forms — Nibchaz a dog, Tartak an ass — but note that Gesenius took Tartak for a demon of the lower regions and Nibchaz for an evil demon of the Zabians, a dreadful king of the infernal world. On Adrammelech and Anammelech they are clear from the child-sacrifice that these were kindred to Moloch, Adrammelech meaning either “glorious king” or, from the Persian, “fire-king,” the name recurring as a personal name at 2 Kings 19:37 and Isaiah 37:38. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke describes an ancient dog-headed image of Nibhaz that lay before him as he wrote, identifies it with the Egyptian Anubis, takes Tartak for the ass or the sun's chariot, and derives Adrammelech from “glorious king” and Anammelech from “returning king,” the Moloch of the Ammonites.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is the most reserved: Nibhaz and Tartak are simply unknown, though the forms have an Assyrio-Babylonian cast, and the Septuagint even inserts another name before them; but Adrammelech and Anammelech he confidently reads as the Assyrian Adar-malik, “Adar is prince,” and Anum-malik, “Anu is prince,” both drawn from well-known Assyrian gods. The witnesses divide again by method, the rabbinic dog-and-ass against the Assyriological restoration; but the verse's own horror overrides the philology. Whatever their forms, the gods of Sepharvaim were served by burning children in the fire — the same Moloch-worship for which Israel had just been condemned in v.17 — so that the heathen who replaced the exiles brought into the land the very abomination that had helped to empty it.
32:
Alongside their own gods the colonists take up the worship of Jehovah too: “so they feared the LORD, and made unto themselves of the lowest of them priests of the high places, which sacrificed for them in the houses of the high places.” The reformed John Gill reads the opening clause as their compliance with the Bethel priest's instruction — they worshipped the God of Israel in the manner they had been taught.

The witnesses divide over the phrase “of the lowest of them.” Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke take it of the meanest sort of people: one priest, Clarke reasons, was not enough for so mixed a population, and since the office was neither respectable nor lucrative, only the lowest of the people would enter it. But the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the rendering to “of all orders” or “promiscuously,” and finds in it yet another mark that it was Jeroboam's mode of worship being restored, comparing the making of priests “of the lowest of the people” at 1 Kings 12:31; the “bamah-priests,” he adds, stand opposed to the priests of Jehovah's temple. So the disagreement is whether the new priesthood was drawn from the dregs of the people, as Gill and Clarke hold, or indifferently from all classes, as Ellicott argues from the parallel.

Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical voice, read the verse in the same key as Ellicott, taking it that the colonists worshipped Jehovah in temples of the high places according to the Israelite priest's instructions, and made themselves priests from the mass of the people who prepared their sacrifices in the high-place houses. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds a fine distinction: these priests sacrificed unto the true God, for as to the worship of their own gods the colonists needed no instruction and would not permit a priest of another religion to officiate. The witnesses do not divide on the substance. Whatever the rank of its priests, the new worship of Jehovah in Samaria was cast in the mould of Jeroboam's calf-religion from the outset — high places, home-made priests, and sacrifices offered where God had never appointed — so that even the true God was received into the land under the forms of the very apostasy for which the land had been emptied.
33:
The historian states the settled result in a single balanced sentence: “they feared the LORD, and served their own gods, after the manner of the nations whom they carried away from thence.” The reformed John Gill puts it in the fewest words — they worshipped both; and in that little phrase lies the whole verdict of the passage.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke exposes the divided heart the verse describes. They did not relinquish their own idolatry, but incorporated the worship of the true God with that of their idols; they were afraid of Jehovah, who had sent the lions among them, and therefore offered him a sort of worship that he might not so afflict them again, while they served their own gods with real devotion, because that service gratified their grossest passions and most sinful propensities. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the Hebrew with a telling emphasis — “Jehovah were they fearing, and their own gods were they serving” — and takes the verse as a recapitulation of all that verses twenty-eight through thirty-two have described; the closing clause he reads as “according to the customs of the cities whence they had been carried away.” Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical voice, likewise treat the verse as summing up by way of conclusion the several kinds of worship the colonists had adopted.

The witnesses do not divide, and their unanimity fastens on the great fault the chapter has been driving toward: not the rejection of Jehovah but the reduction of him to one god among many. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states it exactly — they worshipped God outwardly in the way the Israelites had used, but every nation made gods of its own besides. Clarke's analysis is the sharpest: fear of the true God prompted a prudential, self-protecting worship, while love of the false gods commanded the heart. It is the very sin for which Israel fell, now perpetuated by the strangers who replaced them — a religion that keeps the LORD's rite for safety and its idols for pleasure, and so, as the next verses will insist, does not truly fear him at all.
34:
The historian brings the description down to his own day, and with it a hard word: “unto this day they do after the former manners: they fear not the LORD, neither do they after…the law and commandment which the LORD commanded the children of Jacob, whom he named Israel.” A crux at once confronts the witnesses, for verse thirty-three had just said the colonists “feared the LORD.” The reformed John Gill offers three possible referents for “they”: the new colonies keeping up their former customs, or the Israelites in captivity unreformed by their troubles, or such of the ten tribes as were left in the land.

The grammatical-historical witnesses resolve the tension by distinguishing two senses of “fear.” Charles Ellicott explains that “they fear not the LORD” means they fear him not in the sense of a right fear; they do not honour him in the way he prescribed in the Torah — a true worship, not the mongrel one they kept. He thinks the writer here has in view the remnant of the ten tribes who amalgamated with the settlers, comparing 2 Chronicles 34:6 and John 4:12, and notes that the Septuagint omits both negatives. Keil and Delitzsch read the same way: the mixed cultus of idols and Jehovah was retained until the books were written, and to worship so is not to fear Jehovah at all in the sense of the law he gave to the sons of Jacob, whom he named Israel, as at 1 Kings 18:31.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gathers both the tension and its solution. As the Israelites before their captivity gave these nations an ill example, serving the Lord and Baal together, so the colonists do to this day; and though they pretended to fear both the LORD and their idols, in truth they feared neither the LORD nor worshipped him, but only their own calves and vain inventions — for God will not accept the mongrel worship men offer him beside their idols. Their living in the constant breach of his statutes was itself the proof that, whatever they professed, they did not fear him. So the witnesses turn the apparent contradiction into the chapter's sharpest point: a divided worship is, in the reckoning of God, no fear of God at all.
35:
To measure the colonists' failure — and Israel's before them — the historian recites the ancient covenant they broke: “with whom the LORD had made a covenant, and charged them, saying, Ye shall not fear other gods, nor bow yourselves to them, nor serve them, nor sacrifice to them.” The reformed John Gill fixes the covenant here recalled as the one the LORD made at Sinai, the solemn bond that bound Israel to himself alone.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read verses thirty-five through thirty-nine as a single connected charge, and set out its shape: the covenant forbids the fear, the bowing, the serving, and the sacrificing to other gods, and requires instead that the LORD who brought Israel up out of Egypt with great power and a stretched-out arm be the sole object of their fear, worship, and sacrifice; his statutes and ordinances they are to observe for ever, his covenant they are not to forget, and he in turn will deliver them out of the hand of all their enemies — a charge, they add, that the next verse will record Israel flatly refusing to keep.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gains its whole force from the fourfold prohibition it lays down. The covenant does not merely counsel against idolatry but fences it round on every side: the people are not to fear other gods with the heart, nor bow to them with the body, nor serve them in worship, nor sacrifice to them in offering. Each verb closes a door by which apostasy might enter. And the very first of them — “ye shall not fear other gods” — is the hinge of the whole chapter, for it is precisely the fear divided between Jehovah and the idols that verses thirty-three and thirty-four have exposed in the colonists and that the entire indictment has traced in fallen Israel. By reciting the charge now, at the story's end, the historian lays the covenant like a plumb-line against the crooked wall of Samaria's worship, old and new alike, and shows how far both had leaned from the one command on which the nation's life had hung. The God who redeemed them asked an undivided fear; that undivided fear neither Israel nor her heathen successors would give.
36:
The covenant charge turns from prohibition to its positive ground: “but the LORD, which brought you up out of the land of Egypt with great power and a stretched out arm, him shall ye fear, and him shall ye worship, and to him shall ye do sacrifice.” The reformed John Gill marks why the Exodus is named here — to set before the people the obligation of gratitude under which their redemption had laid them, that the God who saved them might have their whole service.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives the verse its fullest exposition, finding in it three things at once. First, the object of worship: “the LORD” — Jehovah, the supreme, self-existent, and eternal Being, author of all life, who alone is to be adored. Second, the reason of that worship: he had brought them up out of Egypt, saving them by a power so irresistible that in such a Being they might safely confide. Third, the spirit and manner in which it was to be rendered — and this Clarke unfolds phrase by phrase. “Him shall ye fear”: reverence him as Lawgiver and Judge, keeping all his commandments, doing what he enjoins and avoiding what he forbids. “Him shall ye worship”: bow the knee before him, living in the spirit of obedience and performing every religious act in the deepest humility. “To him shall ye do sacrifice”: reckoning that, as they have sinned and deserve death, they must bring their living victims to the altar and let the life-blood be poured out as an atonement for their souls. So Clarke sums the verse in three heads — the object, the reasons, and the manner of true worship, which is to be offered in fear, in humility, and by sacrifice.

The witnesses do not divide, and Gill's note and Clarke's exposition run in one channel. The verse answers the prohibitions of the previous verse with their reason: a single fact grounds the whole exclusive claim, that this God, and no idol of Babylon or Hamath, had reached into Egypt and brought them out with a stretched-out arm.
