Because he redeemed them, he alone may be feared; because his power was irresistible, in him alone may they trust; and the fear, the worship, and the sacrifice that the idols usurped belong of right to him. The colonists and the exiled tribes had divided among many gods a devotion the Exodus had claimed whole for one.
37:
The charge presses on from the person of God to the permanence of his law: “and the statutes, and the ordinances, and the law, and the commandment, which he wrote for you, ye shall observe to do for evermore; and ye shall not fear other gods.” The reformed John Gill offers the one plain gloss the verse needs, that these were the statutes and ordinances the LORD had written for them on the two tables of stone — the law given not by word only but by the finger of God, and set down to endure.

The witnesses give this verse little comment, and honesty asks that the little be drawn out rather than padded. What the verse itself presses is the heaping up of terms — statutes, ordinances, law, and commandment — four words for the one revealed will of God, piled together to leave the people no room to plead that any part of it was optional or unknown. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott had noticed, in weighing the neighbouring verses, that this same fourfold formula recurs as a fixed covenant expression, the pairs standing exegetical of one another; and here they are gathered under a single charge, “ye shall observe to do them for evermore.” The permanence is the point Gill's note quietly underwrites: what God wrote on stone he wrote to last, and what he wrote for them he wrote for their children after them, for evermore.

And the verse ends where the whole charge began, with the refrain “ye shall not fear other gods.” The historian, reciting the covenant against the syncretism of Samaria, will not let that one prohibition drop out of sight for more than a sentence. It framed the charge in verse thirty-five; it closes the demand for obedience here; it will sound again in the verse to follow. The obligation to keep the written law and the ban on fearing other gods are, in the witnesses' reading, two sides of one loyalty: to observe the LORD's statutes for ever is of necessity to refuse every rival deity, and the divided worship of the colonists broke both halves of the covenant at a single stroke. What God had written for ever, Samaria had overwritten with the names of its idols.
38:
The charge sounds its central prohibition once more: “and the covenant that I have made with you ye shall not forget; neither shall ye fear other gods.” The reformed John Gill identifies the covenant not to be forgotten as the law given at Mount Sinai, whose first table chiefly concerned the worship of the one true and living God and forbade the worship of any other — the very heart of the bond now recalled against a people who had forgotten it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays his finger on the feature that governs the whole passage: the clause “neither shall ye fear other gods” is repeated three times over — in verse thirty-five, in verse thirty-seven, and here in verse thirty-eight — as the main point of the covenant between Jehovah and Israel. The threefold hammer-stroke is the historian's own emphasis, and the witnesses let it carry the weight of the verse.

For the repetition is not accident but argument. The covenant had many statutes and ordinances, as verse thirty-seven had piled them up; yet the historian returns, again and again, to this single command, because it is the one that Samaria's whole religion had broken. To “not forget the covenant” is, first and last, to fear no other god beside the LORD; and the mixed worship of the colonists — Jehovah kept for fear of the lions, the idols kept for love — was precisely a forgetting of the covenant at its central clause. Gill's note on the first table and Ellicott's note on the threefold refrain converge on one truth: that of all the LORD had commanded, the exclusive fear of himself stood first, and that the sin the chapter has anatomized from verse seven onward, in Israel and in her heathen successors alike, was at bottom a single sin against a single, thrice-repeated word. The historian will not let it be lost among the rest. Three times in four verses he sets it down, so that no reader of Samaria's ruin can mistake the commandment on which that ruin turned. And Gill's observation that the first table of the Sinai law was itself given chiefly to secure the worship of the one true God, forbidding every other, only deepens the point: to fear other gods was not to break some lesser statute but to overthrow the covenant at its very head, the first word of the first table, on which all the rest depended.
39:
The covenant charge closes with a promise attached to obedience: “but the LORD your God ye shall fear; and he shall deliver you out of the hand of all your enemies.” The reformed John Gill takes “fear” here in its full compass, as the worship of God both inwardly and outwardly according to his revealed will, for the fear of God includes both the internal and the external service of him.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches the force of the Hebrew in a single stroke: the pronoun is emphatic — “and He, on his part, will deliver you.” The stress falls on the LORD as the one who acts: let the people render him their whole fear, and he for his part will do the delivering. The verse thus binds a demand and a pledge into one sentence, the fear required and the deliverance promised answering each to the other.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rounds off the recited covenant on a note of grace that throws the chapter's whole tragedy into relief. Here was the bargain God had offered his people: an undivided fear, and in return deliverance out of the hand of all their enemies. The irony the historian intends is unmistakable when the verse is read against the story it closes. Israel had just been delivered into the hand of her enemies, carried to Assyria; and the colonists who replaced her cowered before lions in the land. The one condition of rescue — to fear the LORD alone — was the very thing neither the exiled nation nor the heathen settlers would meet. Ellicott's emphatic “He” only sharpens the point: the God who alone could deliver stood ready to deliver, if only he were feared alone; but a fear parcelled out among the gods of Babylon and Hamath forfeited the promise even as it broke the command. The covenant held out safety in the hand of the one God; Samaria, old and new, chose instead the crowded and helpless protection of many, and found no deliverer among them all. And Gill's insistence that the fear here required embraces both the inward reverence of the heart and the outward acts of worship guards the promise against the very fault that undid them: the colonists rendered God an outward rite while their hearts ran after their idols, and such a halved and hollow fear could never claim the whole deliverance that the whole fear alone was promised.
40:
The recited covenant meets its flat refusal: “howbeit they did not hearken, but they did after their former manner.” The reformed John Gill reads the verdict without softening: they did not repent of their idolatries but persisted in them — even when they were in captivity in Assyria, or such of them as were left in the land. The charge had been full and the promise generous, and the answer was simply that they would not hear.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the reference of the pronoun: “they” are the Ephraimites; “did” means they continued doing; and “after their former manner” means they clung to the old-established cultus of the calves. The verse, on his reading, looks back past the whole Samaritan settlement to the original sin of the northern kingdom, and finds it unbroken to the last.

The witnesses do not divide, and the brevity of the verse is itself eloquent. After the long and solemn rehearsal of the covenant — the fourfold prohibition, the Exodus ground, the written law to be kept for ever, the thrice-sounded charge to fear no other god, the very promise of deliverance — the historian records the response in a single weary clause: they did not hearken. It is the same word that fell in verse fourteen, where the fathers “would not hear” the prophets; the chapter ends as it had run, with a people deaf to God. Gill lets the refusal reach even into the exile, so that neither the captives in Assyria nor the remnant in the land was reformed by all that had befallen them; Ellicott traces it to the calves of Jeroboam, the sin from which, as verse twenty-two had said, Israel “departed not.” The covenant offered a way of life and the people kept their former manner — and in that one refusal, quietly restated here at the close, lies the whole reason for the ruin the chapter has spent forty verses explaining. And the verdict on the exiles matches the verdict on the strangers who took their land: the same deaf and divided heart in both, the same refusal to let even judgment teach what mercy and warning could not, so that neither the old people carried to Assyria nor the new colony settled in Samaria would be brought to hear the God whose one command was that they should hear him alone.
41:
The chapter ends where it has long been tending, in a worship forever divided: “so these nations feared the LORD, and served their graven images, both their children, and their children's children: as did their fathers, so do they unto this day.” The reformed John Gill draws the exact parallel that seals the whole: the nations feared the LORD and served their graven images just as the Israelites had done before them, who served the Lord and the calves, and worshipped God and Baal together. The heathen successors had inherited not only Israel's land but Israel's very sin.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the phrase “unto this day” as a mark of date: it must have been written before the Babylonish captivity, he argues, because after that time none of the Israelites ever lapsed into idolatry; and he refers the words chiefly to the heathen sent to dwell among the remains of the ten tribes. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same phrase to the opposite conclusion. Calling the verse a variation of v.33, he observes that the captivity of Ephraim took place in 721 B.C., and that “their children, and their children's children” — two generations later — brings us down to the times of the exile of Judah, the age of the last redactor of Kings. So the witnesses divide over the composition of the book: Clarke dating the note before the fall of Judah, Ellicott two generations after the fall of Samaria, in the days of the final compiler.

On the theology of the ending they are wholly one, and the chapter's long argument gathers into its last clause. Keil and Delitzsch simply let the verse stand as the summary it is — these nations feared the LORD and served their images, as their fathers did, so do they to this day. The divided worship the whole chapter has anatomized, the fear of Jehovah wedded to the service of idols, is shown at the close to be no passing phase but a settled inheritance, handed down through children and children's children. The land that God emptied for such a worship he refilled with a people who practised the same; and the historian lays down his pen on a Samaria that fears the LORD and serves its graven images still — a standing monument to the sin for which Israel fell.
